theme: Silence has receipts — give every empty thing its data story
visual_observations: Desktop Office: 6 desks total in 2 rows of 3, only Maya (top-row-left) and Dave (top-row-center) desks glow active with lit outlines; bottom row (Carlos, Dave-2, Sofia) is dim and ghosted. Bloberto centered top as CVO with 'crushing' face. 'COFFEE TRIPLE SHOT' corner top-right is static at 2-person capacity — over-caffeinated for the crowd. 'MIDNIGHT' time widget right-side is a tiny dark box. Conference ring bottom-left still floats unlabeled and empty. 'everyone is at their desk' footer text visible bottom-center. Desktop Dashboard: Stats bar shows ON THE CLOCK 2, ACTIVE 2 'dynamic duo', IDLE 0 'all hands on deck', LIVE 01:34:48 AM, TEAM VIBE Crushing It with a 2-dot sparkline. Both Maya and Dave have Bug task tags and near-full progress bars. NO mention of Carlos, Sofia, or Luna anywhere in the dashboard — they've been fully erased. Header reads '☕ Good morning, LEGEND 🔥' at 1:34 AM which is factually wrong. Activity log filter shows per-worker tabs (Maya, Carlos, Dave, Sofia, Luna) but the log itself is below the fold. Mobile: Office view compressed but functional — Bloberto, Maya, Dave labeled; lower-row workers visible but small; 'TEAM LOCKED IN' vibe caption banner at bottom (transient toast caught mid-display); footer text just legible.

ideas.title: 1:34 AM Is Not Good Morning
ideas.description: In App.jsx around line 1001, the greeting ternary has three brackets: `currentHour < 12` → '☕ Good morning', `< 18` → '🌤️ Good afternoon', else → '🌙 Good evening'. At 1:34 AM (hour=1), `< 12` fires and the header cheerfully says 'Good morning' next to a 🔥 Crushing It vibe badge. It's a 3AM coding session — this is tone-deaf. Prepend one condition: `currentHour < 5` → '🦉 Still at it' (or '🌃 Burning the midnight oil'). The HONORIFICS map already has `'after-hours': 'night owl 🌙'` — the greeting and honorific should agree. One line of code fixes a jarring contradiction that any visitor at midnight will notice immediately.

ideas.title: The Dashboard Has No Memory of Absent Workers
ideas.description: The Dashboard shows 'Active Workers (2)' with zero trace of Carlos, Sofia, or Luna. The floor correctly renders them as ghost desks using `ghostRoster` (computed by diffing `DEFAULT_ROSTER` against active worker IDs in Office.jsx). The Dashboard needs to mirror this. Below the active workers grid, add a collapsible 'Off the Clock (3)' section: minimal cards showing each ghost worker's emoji, name, role, and last-seen timestamp from the most recent activity log entry. The data is all present — `DEFAULT_ROSTER` in constants.js + `activityEntries` filtered by name. Without this section, the dashboard implies the team has 2 people. With it, users understand the bench, see who might return, and the empty floor makes emotional sense.

ideas.title: Dispatch Labels That Know They're Navigation
ideas.description: The 'dynamic duo' text in the ACTIVE stat card (StatsBar in App.jsx) is dead text. The 'Stuck' stat card already has a model: `onClick` calls `document.querySelector('[data-worker-name=\"...\"]')?.scrollIntoView()`. Apply the same pattern to the ACTIVE card's dispatch label area — clicking 'dynamic duo' (or 'lone wolf' or 'skeleton crew') should scroll to and briefly focus-ring the `.workers-grid` section. Add `cursor: pointer` and `title='Jump to workers'` on the dispatch span. This makes the stats bar interactive rather than decorative, creating a natural flow from summary → detail that currently doesn't exist.

ideas.title: Ghost Desks Need to Speak Their Last Task
ideas.description: In Office.jsx, `deskOccupants` marks ghost workers with `{ ghost: true }`. These desks render as quiet, desaturated characters. But each ghost worker has a data trail: their last `task` string and last activity timestamp exist in `activityEntries` (already passed as a prop to Office). Render a tiny Post-it SVG absolutely-positioned over the ghost desk (top-right corner of the desk cell, ~22×18px, yellow fill, slight rotation) showing the task truncated to 18 chars. On hover, expand to a popover with full task + relative time ('Sofia · spec coverage · 3h ago'). This transforms every empty desk from a hole in the floor to a frozen moment. The office becomes an archaeology site — you can feel the day that was.

ideas.title: WallClock Is AWOL on Mobile
ideas.description: The `<WallClock vibeKey={vibe} />` component renders at an absolute position inside `.office-floor` that gets clipped on 375px viewports. Looking at the mobile screenshot, the clock is completely absent — the most atmospheric real-time element in the office just vanishes. In Office.css, add a mobile media query that repositions `.wall-clock` to `top: 6px; right: 8px; transform: scale(0.75)` at `max-width: 480px`. The clock is already vibe-reactive (it renders custom face copy for on-fire: 'Time has no meaning during an incident') — that storytelling is being lost on every mobile visit.

ideas.title: Vibe Streak Needs a Eulogy
ideas.description: The streak badge system (vibeStreak >= 3 shows 👑, >= 5 shows 🔥, >= 10 shows 🏆) celebrates streaks beautifully on the way up. But when a streak breaks (vibeStreak was > 3 and resets to 0), nothing happens — the badge just disappears silently. Add a `prevStreakRef` (useRef) that tracks the previous streak value. When it drops from > 3 to ≤ 1, fire a `streakEulogy` toast (same pattern as `completionToast` in App.jsx): '💀 RIP the streak — it was beautiful while it lasted' for 3.5s. Use a muted border-color (var(--text-muted)) to differentiate grief from celebration. The streak eulogy makes users care about maintaining the streak precisely because its loss is acknowledged.

ideas.title: Vibe Sparkline First-Run Is a 2-Dot Shrug
ideas.description: The TEAM VIBE stat card's VibeSparkline shows only 2 connected dots on first visit because `VIBE_HISTORY_KEY` in localStorage is empty and history is being built live (max 12 entries). A 2-dot sparkline looks like a broken chart, not a feature. When `vibeHistory.length < 3`, replace the SVG with a gentle skeleton: an animated shimmer line (CSS `@keyframes shimmer`) with text 'Building vibe history…' in `var(--text-muted)` at 0.7rem. Once history has 3+ entries, switch to the real sparkline. This prevents the first-visit impression that the chart is broken, and makes the sparkline feel like it was earned once it appears.

ideas.title: The Conference Table Should Be a War Room
ideas.description: The conference table (`<ConferenceTable>` in Office.jsx) already has `data-meeting` and a fire-glow SVG filter activated when `meetingWorkers.length >= 2` wander to it during idle. But this is passive — the table has no identity of its own. Map it to the most recent team-wide event in `activityEntries`: when the last entry is a `complete` type, render a tiny confetti SVG burst on the table surface (3-4 colored dots using the completing worker's `ROLE_COLORS`); when it's an `error` type, render a red warning diamond; when it's `hire`, render a 🎉 badge. Add a `title` attribute to the `.conference-table` div showing 'Last team event: {message} · {relativeTime}'. The table goes from ambient decoration to a ticker tape of team history — a physical activity feed that doesn't require navigating away from the floor.

theme: Every prop has a story — stop leaving the good ones on the floor
visual_observations: Desktop office view at 4:04 AM — Bloberto nameplate reads CVO, Sofia is the lone worker visible with a nameplate reading SOMA under her character, the ficus orb glows bottom-left, coffee corner shows COFFEE TRIPLE SHOT in top-right, the MIDNIGHT terminal panel sits dormant top-right. Dashboard shows Sofia in solo-spotlight mode: QA Engineer, 5% progress, 19 minutes in, Design + QA tags. Stats bar: 1 On the Clock, 1 Active (lone wolf), 0 Idle (all hands on deck), LIVE 04:04:34 AM, Team Vibe: Crushing It with vibe sparkline showing only 2 connected dots. Mobile: stats cards stack into a clean 2-column grid, but the ShortcutToast bar renders fixed-bottom and physically covers Sofia task description text at the bottom of her card.

ideas.title: Shortcut Bar Does a Drive-By on Mobile
ideas.description: The ShortcutToast component (App.jsx around line 590-680) uses position fixed at bottom, which on a 375px viewport renders directly over the Activity Log and the lower portion of the active WorkerCard. In the mobile screenshot, it literally covers Sofia task description. Fix: add a media query in App.css for max-width 640px to push the toast up (bottom: 4rem, smaller font), or suppress it entirely on touch-only devices since keyboard shortcuts are irrelevant there. WHY: this is not cosmetic — it hides data a user came to see.

ideas.title: VIBE_WEATHER_LABELS Is Haunting the Codebase
ideas.description: Office.jsx defines VIBE_WEATHER_LABELS and VIBE_WEATHER_ICONS near line 95-109 — five beautifully written vibe-to-weather metaphors: Clear skies team at full strength, Storm conditions all hands, Night sky the stars are watching. They are never referenced anywhere in the render tree. This is finished creative work sitting in dead code. Surface it: render the weather icon and label as a small status line just below the office-sign div in Office.jsx. Alternatively use it as a title tooltip on the vibe pill in the header. Either path takes one JSX line. WHY: this copy is too good to waste and would instantly add world-building to the office.

ideas.title: Activity Log Count Lies When a Filter Is Active
ideas.description: In App.jsx, the section header above the ActivityLog reads Last N events where N is Math.min(activityLog.length, 20). This number comes from the full unfiltered array — but the entries prop passed to ActivityLog is already filtered by activityFilter and workerFilter. So when a user clicks Errors and only 2 entries match, the header still declares Last 20 events. Fix: compute the displayed count from the filtered and sliced array before passing to ActivityLog, and update the header text to reflect the real count with a (filtered) suffix when a filter is active. WHY: the header is the trust anchor — if it lies, the whole feed feels broken.

ideas.title: Bloberto C-Suite Promotion Deserves a Moment
ideas.description: In Office.jsx, BLOBERTO_TITLES maps each vibe to a C-suite alias: CVO, CFO, CISO, CDO, CPO. The mgr-desk__nameplate reflects the current title but the nameplate is about 30px wide with 5 characters of text tucked under the manager desk — completely invisible unless you are looking for it. The existing vibeCaption state is already wired to show a floating announcement for 2.8s on vibe change (useEffect around line 1140 of Office.jsx). Extend that effect: append the new title to the caption, for example Team locked in — Bloberto assumes role of CVO. Zero new state needed, one string concat. WHY: this is the kind of micro-narrative that makes people screenshot the app and send it to their team.

ideas.title: The Whiteboard Calls It an Average With One Person in the Room
ideas.description: In Office.jsx, avgProgress is always labeled avg task progress on the whiteboard including when workingWorkers.length equals 1. Averaging a single number is not an average, it is the number. The wb-progress-label span renders that text unconditionally around line 1265. Change it to a ternary: when workingWorkers.length is 1 render task progress, otherwise avg task progress. One line, one ternary, genuine data integrity. WHY: Sofia is carrying the whole team right now — her 5% is her own and the whiteboard should honor that without the word avg.

ideas.title: Sofia Desk Monitor Should Know She Is at 5%
ideas.description: The DESK_SCREEN_SVG object in Office.jsx has handcrafted mini-screens for every character. Sofia entry shows four static green bars at fixed heights. But worker.progress is real data. The bar height and y attributes could be driven by a CSS custom property: pass style with --progress set to worker.progress divided by 100 on the desk-monitor-screen wrapper and update the Sofia SVG rects to calculate height from that variable. At 5% the bars would be nearly flat; at 100% they reach the ceiling. Scope this to the sofia and sofia_error keys first. WHY: it turns pure decoration into data and would be the most satisfying 32x16px dashboard ever shipped.

ideas.title: Tag Filter Has No Emergency Exit Sign
ideas.description: In App.jsx, selectedTag controls card-level filtering. When active, all non-matching WorkerCards receive worker-card--dimmed. The only way to clear the filter is to re-click the active tag pill on a specific card which shows a times symbol when active (task-tag render around line 290). There is no global indicator in the section-header above workers-grid that a filter is even active. Add a dismissible chip between the section title and the right-side status text showing the active filter name with an x to clear it. This chip renders only when selectedTag is not null and calls setSelectedTag(null) on click. WHY: tag filtering is a power feature that becomes a trap for anyone who discovers it by accident.

ideas.title: The Ficus Has Achieved Sentience and Nobody Told the Visitors
ideas.description: In Office.jsx, plantStage evolves through five named stages from seedling to blooming driven by vibeStreak. There is a plantToast that fires on level-up. But the plant sits on the office floor as an emoji with no discoverable identity. A new visitor has no idea it is alive, what stage it is at, or that their team vibe is literally growing it. Add a hover and tap interaction anchored to the ficus element: a small card reading Office Ficus, Stage: [current plantStageLabel], Grows when the vibe holds, and the current streak count. Style it like the existing vacant-job-card CSS which already handles this floating-card pattern. Use the existing plantStageLabel and vibeStreak props already available in Office.jsx. WHY: this is the most charming systemic storytelling in the entire app — it deserves to be discovered, not missed.

theme: The invisible infrastructure — surface the systems that silently power the experience
visual_observations: Reviewed via code analysis — Puppeteer timed out in container. Key observations from code: polling pauses after IDLE_TIMEOUT but no user-facing indicator; cinematic entry/exit system (fadingMap, newIds, doorEvent) exists but door events have no sound or haptic feedback; vibeHistory stored in localStorage but never exported; quoteBonus system exists but discovery path is unclear; theme toggle exists but only dark/light with no vibe-reactive theming.

ideas.title: Polling Paused State Is Invisible to the User
ideas.description: When IDLE_TIMEOUT fires, pollingPaused becomes true and the fetch interval stretches from 30s to 60s — but the UI gives zero indication. The LIVE badge in the stats bar keeps glowing green. Users who leave the tab open (the exact behavior we want to encourage) come back to stale data with no way to know. When pollingPaused is true, change the LIVE badge to show 'PAUSED' with a muted amber color and add a click handler that calls touch() to resume. One conditional className swap on the existing badge element. WHY: trust in a live dashboard depends on knowing when it's NOT live.

ideas.title: Door Events Deserve Sound Design
ideas.description: The doorEvent state already fires a 'worker-entering'/'worker-leaving' animation with the worker's name and emoji visible for a few seconds. But it's purely visual — in a background tab you miss it entirely. Add an optional Web Audio API chime: a short ascending tone for arrivals, descending for departures. Gate it behind a localStorage preference 'bloberto-sound' (default off) with a small speaker icon toggle next to the theme toggle. WHY: the office metaphor is spatial — sound completes the illusion. Background tab users would hear their team arrive.

ideas.title: Vibe History Export — Let Teams Share Their Journey
ideas.description: vibeHistory accumulates in localStorage via VIBE_HISTORY_KEY but there's no way to export or share it. Add a small download icon on the VibeSparkline component that exports vibeHistory as a simple CSV (timestamp, vibe, workerCount). People love sharing team stats. This also future-proofs against localStorage being cleared. WHY: the sparkline tells a story — but right now it's a story that dies when you clear your browser.

ideas.title: Theme Should Follow the Vibe, Not Just a Toggle
ideas.description: The theme state is binary dark/light, stored in localStorage. But the vibe system already knows if the team is crushing-it, on-fire, or chill. Map each vibe to subtle CSS custom property overrides — on-fire gets warmer accent colors (oranges, reds in --accent), chill gets cooler blues, skeleton-crew gets muted grays. Keep the dark/light base but layer vibe-reactive accent shifts using data-vibe attribute on documentElement alongside data-theme. WHY: the office already reacts to vibe emotionally (captions, emojis) but the color palette stays static — the biggest visual surface is untouched.

ideas.title: The Quote Bonus System Has No Discovery Path
ideas.description: quoteBonus and showBonus states exist but the mechanism to trigger them is buried. There's a footer sparkle for first-time visitors (sparkleUnseen) but once dismissed, the bonus system becomes invisible. Add a subtle '?' tooltip on the footer that explains 'Click for a team quote — collect them all!' with a count of unique quotes seen (stored in localStorage). Gamification only works when players know there's a game. WHY: the quote system is already built — it just needs a front door.

ideas.title: Conference Table Should Show Meeting Duration
ideas.description: When meetingWorkers.length >= 2 triggers the conference table glow in Office.jsx, there's no sense of how long the meeting has been going. Add a small timer that starts counting when the meeting begins (tracked via a useRef that captures the timestamp when meetingWorkers crosses from <2 to >=2). Display it as a tiny 'mm:ss' under the table. WHY: meetings are the universal office experience — showing duration adds comedy ('oh god they've been in there 47 minutes') and relatability.

ideas.title: Worker Card Flip Should Have Momentum
ideas.description: The isFlipped state on WorkerCard toggles instantly. The CSS presumably has a transform: rotateY transition, but there's no haptic or bounce. Add a cubic-bezier overshoot to the flip transition (e.g., cubic-bezier(0.68, -0.55, 0.265, 1.55)) and a very subtle box-shadow shift during the mid-flip point using a transitionend listener to add/remove a 'flipping' class. WHY: physicality in UI creates emotional resonance — the card should feel like it has weight.

ideas.title: Office Ambient Soundscape Generator
ideas.description: Build a minimal ambient sound layer using Web Audio API oscillators and noise — not audio files. Keyboard typing sounds (filtered white noise bursts) proportional to active worker count. Subtle coffee machine hiss when someone is in the coffee corner. Gentle hum that shifts pitch with team vibe. All procedurally generated, all optional (same sound toggle as the door chimes idea). The office already has visual life — giving it a sonic presence would make it genuinely meditative to keep open. Think lofi-beats-to-code-to but generated from real team data. WHY: no other team dashboard has ever tried this, and it would be the single most shareable feature.

theme: Every transition is a confession — make the invisible state changes say something
visual_observations: Desktop office at 4:04 AM Monday Mar 9: Bloberto centered top as CVO with a speech bubble reading 'hour?? Legends.' — the honorific correctly fires 'night owl 🌙' in the header confirming the after-hours fix from #59 landed. Sofia is the lone working character, nameplate reads 'SOFIA' in active uppercase styling, magnifying glass visible on her SVG. All other desks (Carlos, Maya, Dave, Luna) are ghosted and dim. Conference table at bottom-center shows the 'crushing' 6-chair ring layout with 'everyone is at their desk' ambient label in tiny text — chairs render as a beautiful purple ring of dots around the round table. COFFEE TRIPLE SHOT label top-right. MIDNIGHT panel (the office window time caption) visible as a dark terminal-style box. Vibe weather ☀️ icon bottom-right floats near the cactus with no label. Three decorative plants scattered: green plant at left edge, tropical plant at right-mid, cactus at right. Bottom-left has ambient particle dots. Shortcut toast visible in bottom-right as a pill. Mobile dashboard at same timestamp: stats stack into 2-column grid cleanly. ON THE CLOCK: 1, ACTIVE: 1 (lone wolf), IDLE: 0 (all hands on deck), LIVE: 04:04:38 AM, TEAM VIBE: Crushing It with sparkline showing 3 dots and a legend trail. Sofia solo-spotlight card renders full-width. The solo-spotlight caption reads the single static string about 'one pair of hands'. Keyboard shortcut bar renders fixed-bottom, still overlapping task text on Sofia's card.

ideas.title: Polling Paused Pill Sounds Like a System Error, Not an Office
ideas.description: The pollingPaused pill in App.jsx renders '⏸ Paused · move mouse to refresh' — the single most utilitarian string in a product that otherwise breathes with vibe-aware copy everywhere. When someone is 'Crushing It' at 4AM and the app pauses after 10 minutes of inactivity, this message sounds like a DevTools warning, not a moment in Bloberto's world. Every other idle/waiting state has flavor: the WallClock says 'Still here? So am I. I am a clock. That is fine for me.' for after-hours, the empty state says 'The office is quiet... for now 🌙'. Create a `POLLING_PAUSED_COPY` constant object keyed by vibeKey with messages like: crushing → '⏸ Bloberto stepped away · nudge the mouse to resume', on-fire → '⏸ Paused mid-incident · move to rejoin', slow-day → '⏸ Nothing to see here · which tracks', after-hours → '⏸ The office dozed off · tap to wake it'. The state and aria-live are already wired — this is pure copy, zero logic.

ideas.title: Office Tab Is a Data Blind Spot
ideas.description: When you are on the Office tab, there is zero indication of when data was last synced or whether you are looking at live or stale data. The Dashboard tab has the full LIVE stat card with a real-time timestamp (04:04:38 AM). The Office tab has nothing. If the network drops after you load the office floor, Bloberto could be running a 30-minute-old snapshot and you would have no way to know. The Office component already receives `isSyncing` as a prop — it even uses it to show the typing-dots above Bloberto's head during sync. Extend this: add a tiny sync status indicator anchored to the '🏢 Bloberto's HQ' sign div in Office.jsx. A green dot + 'live' or a pulsing grey dot + 'syncing…' (same styling as `.live-dot` in the stats bar, already defined in App.css). On hover, show a tooltip with the exact lastSynced timestamp. Thread `lastSynced` through the Office props alongside the existing `isSyncing`. WHY: the floor is the feature — it should be trustworthy and tell you so.

ideas.title: Solo Spotlight Has One Line and One Emotion
ideas.description: When exactly one worker is active, App.jsx renders a special `workers-grid--solo` layout with a hardcoded caption: '🌟 Running solo today. Carrying the whole team on one pair of hands.' This is sweet but it fires identically whether the worker is at 5% progress and 4AM or at 95% and nearly done. There is one message for every possible lone wolf reality. Add a `SOLO_SPOTLIGHT_MSGS` object keyed by vibeKey and progress bucket — at minimum, vary by vibe: crushing → '🔥 One person. All the velocity. Every log line earned.', on-fire → '🧯 Holding the line alone. The incident board has one name.', slow-day → '☁️ Everybody else clocked out. The office still has a heartbeat.', after-hours → '🌙 The stars are watching. So are the servers.', in-flow → '⚡ Deep work. No committee required.' These messages cost nothing and change the emotional register completely. The existing caption render is one JSX line with a hardcoded string — swap it for a lookup.

ideas.title: System Events Are Invisible in the Activity Log
ideas.description: In App.jsx, every vibe transition injects a system event into activityLog: `{ type: 'system', message: '🌡️ Vibe: Slow Day → Crushing It', ... }`. These are timestamp-accurate records of the team's energy arc — genuinely the most interesting events in the log. But the activity filter bar only offers All / 🟢 Hires / ✅ Completions / ❌ Errors. System events have no dedicated filter. They are buried inside 'All' and get pushed down as other events arrive. Add a '⚙️ System' option to the filter `[{ key: 'all' }, ...]` array in App.jsx. The ACTIVITY_ICONS object already maps 'system' to '⚙️'. The filter logic is one conditional: `if (activityFilter === 'system') return e.type === 'system'`. WHY: the vibe timeline is the most honest narrative of what the team experienced today — users deserve a way to read it.

ideas.title: Error Recovery Gets No Ceremony
ideas.description: In App.jsx, the `justShipped` detection watches for `working → idle` transitions and fires a '🎉 {name} shipped it!' completionToast with confetti. This is wonderful. But `error → working` — when a worker fixes the incident and gets back to it — fires nothing at all. This is the second most significant status transition in the product and it is completely silent. Add a `justRecovered` detection in the same activeWorkers diff useEffect: `prevMap.get(w.id)?.status === 'error' && w.status === 'working'`. Trigger a toast variant with copy like '🔧 {name} patched it up!' styled with an orange border (ROLE_COLORS['Engineer'] or simply '#f97316') to visually distinguish recovery from completion. Use the existing completionToastTimerRef pattern — no new state infrastructure needed. WHY: celebrating the recovery closes the emotional loop the error opened. Without it, the incident just silently disappears.

ideas.title: The Door Has Amnesia
ideas.description: The door event system in App.jsx (`doorEvent` state set to 'arrive'/'depart' for 1.5s/2s) is genuinely theatrical: the door icon flips to '🚪✨' with a notify dot on arrival. But once the animation fades, all record of it is gone. Nobody knows Sofia arrived 8 minutes ago or that Dave left 40 minutes ago without checking the Activity Log. Add a `doorLog` state array (max 3 entries, managed alongside the existing `doorEvent` state) that appends `{ name, type, ts }` entries from the same appeared/disappeared diffs in the activeWorkers useEffect. Render these as a tiny strip below or beside the `office-door` element in Office.jsx: faded ghost-style avatars with name and relative time ('Sofia · 8m ago ↑', 'Dave · 40m ago ↓'). Fade entries out after 30 minutes. WHY: the door is the only physical threshold in the office — it should carry memory.

ideas.title: The Version String Is the Only Thing Not Alive
ideas.description: The footer renders 'v1.0.0-chaos' as a hardcoded string literal on line 1306 of App.jsx. Every other footer element is dynamic: the tagline rotates by vibe and by user click, the sparkle animates on first visit, the quote counter tracks state. The version string just sits there, unchanged regardless of whether the team is on fire or running legendarily. Add a `VIBE_VERSIONS` constant: crushing → 'v2.0.0-legendary', 'on-fire' → 'v0.9.1-HOTFIX', 'in-flow' → 'v1.0.0-smooth', 'slow-day' → 'v0.0.1-maybe?', 'after-hours' → 'v1.0.0-🌙'. Swap the hardcoded string for `{VIBE_VERSIONS[teamVibe.key] ?? 'v1.0.0-chaos'}`. WHY: the footer is the last thing you read before leaving. Let it close the story instead of breaking it.

ideas.title: Hire a Vibe Archaeologist
ideas.description: The vibeHistory in localStorage grows up to 12 entries and powers the VibeSparkline. The `activityLog` captures vibe transitions as system events. But nobody has ever looked at this data and asked: what does a Bloberto Office session actually look like? We need a Vibe Archaeologist — a rotating role (not a permanent hire) that reads the raw localStorage keys from real sessions, maps vibe sequences to team outcomes (errors tend to follow slow-day more than in-flow), and writes the findings as new VIBE_QUOTES additions or BONUS_TAGLINES that reflect real team patterns. The role would audit once per quarter and submit a PR with 3–5 new quotes sourced from actual vibe data. WHY: Bloberto is a live instrument — the people who watch it have data. The creative system should feed back from that observation. Assign to Luna as first rotation.

theme: Your characters have faces — now give them handwriting
visual_observations: Puppeteer timed out; thorough code review conducted instead. The codebase has invested enormous effort in individual character identity: each team member has a bespoke hand-drawn SVG portrait (Carlos with thick-rimmed glasses, Dave with headphone band, Sofia with magnifying glass, Maya with bicolor pink/purple hair, Luna with crescent moon accessory) and a unique desk screen SVG (Carlos: blue editor lines; Maya: purple wireframe boxes; Dave: orange blinking terminal cursor; Sofia: green bar charts; Luna: indigo Kanban columns with gold star). PING_REACTIONS in Office.jsx nails each voice precisely. But the moment a character goes idle, they lose all individuality: AWAY_MESSAGES ('Coffee run', 'Headphones in') are shared across everyone and indexed by wander slot position, not by who the person is. IDLE_BUBBLE_EMOJIS is keyed by wanderIdx (1-4), not worker.id. ERROR_BUBBLE_MESSAGES fires identically for all five characters regardless of role -- Carlos the backend engineer and Maya the designer both panic with frontend-flavored errors. LONE_SURVIVOR_LABELS are purely vibe-keyed. VACANT_ADS are vibe-keyed but role-agnostic. The card flip back face has no character voice at all -- just timestamped log entries under a name label. The entire characterization architecture lives in the SVG portrait layer; the behavioral and text layer below it reverts to generic chorus. The desk screens -- the most quietly expressive feature in the office scene -- are completely static and decorative.

ideas.title: Away Notes Should Know Whose Desk They Are On
ideas.description: In Office.jsx, the AwaySign component derives its message from AWAY_MESSAGES indexed by (seed + Math.floor(idleMinutes / 10)) mod length -- a shared array of 6 generic strings shared by every worker. Sofia the QA analyst and Dave the backend dev leave identical notes. Replace with a WORKER_AWAY_MESSAGES object keyed by worker.id, falling back to the generic array: carlos -> ['In the logs again', 'Blame attribution in progress', 'Something smells wrong'], maya -> ['Redlining this', 'Making it purple', 'Grid theory break'], dave -> ['Full send in cans', 'Compiling the vibe', 'Cleaning up the diff'], sofia -> ['Found something', 'Writing the spec', 'Lunch (spotted 2 bugs)'], luna -> ['Horizon scanning', 'Staring at this layout', 'Revising the deck']. The AwaySign component already has the workerId prop threading through -- swap AWAY_MESSAGES for a per-worker lookup. WHY: the away note is the most readable character moment in the idle state. It should sound like them, not like a shared office template.

ideas.title: Error Panics Should Match the Panic-er's Job
ideas.description: In Office.jsx, ERROR_BUBBLE_MESSAGES fires for all workers in error state: ['it was fine locally', 'undefined is not a function', 'send help']. Every message is frontend JS developer flavored. When Sofia (QA) panics she says 'undefined is not a function'. When Luna (Creative Director) panics she says 'it was fine locally.' These are wrong by role. Replace the shared array with a WORKER_ERROR_BUBBLES map keyed by worker.id: carlos -> ['SIGKILL', 'db is lying to me', 'logs on fire', '500'], maya -> ['every border is wrong', 'the prototype lied', 'my components are crying'], dave -> ['git blame moment', 'npm ci again?', 'the build is sentient'], sofia -> ['the spec never said this', 'REGRESSION', '5 new bugs found', 'who merged this'], luna -> ['the deck is burning', 'every color is wrong', 'the brief changed again']. Fall back to ERROR_BUBBLE_MESSAGES for workers without a mapping. WHY: panic reveals character -- a QA engineer and a backend dev do not have the same flavor of crisis.

ideas.title: The Card Back Is a Ledger With No Author
ideas.description: In App.jsx, WorkerCard's back face renders the worker name then immediately maps over workerHistory as plain log entries. There is no character voice anywhere on the back. The back should open with a character-specific tagline: a styled .worker-card__back-tagline element drawn from a WORKER_TAGLINES map keyed by worker.id. Examples: carlos -> 'The logs don’t lie.', maya -> 'Designs ship when they’re ready. Sometimes sooner.', dave -> 'Every merged PR had a story.', sofia -> 'Quality or nothing.', luna -> 'Creative velocity is still velocity.' Render it as a small italic/muted line between .worker-card__back-title and the history entries in the WorkerCard back div. WHY: you flip the card to learn something about the person -- you should hear them before you read the receipts.

ideas.title: Idle Micro-Bubbles Know the Seat, Not the Person
ideas.description: In Office.jsx, IDLE_BUBBLE_EMOJIS is { 1: '☕', 2: '💬', 3: '🪟', 4: '🧘' }, indexed by wanderIdx -- the character's wander animation slot (1-4), which is assigned by position order each render, not by identity. Carlos might be wanderIdx=2 one session and wanderIdx=3 the next. Replace with WORKER_IDLE_BUBBLES keyed by worker.id: carlos -> ['📊', '☕', '🐛'], maya -> ['🎨', '💜', '✏️'], dave -> ['🎧', '🎵', '🎤'], sofia -> ['🔍', '📋', '✅'], luna -> ['🌙', '✦', '📔']. Cycle using (Math.floor(Date.now() / 12000)) mod workerBubbles.length. In the Character component, swap IDLE_BUBBLE_EMOJIS[wanderIdx] for the worker-specific lookup. WHY: thought bubbles are windows into someone's inner monologue -- they should not be accidentally assigned by which slot the character happened to land in this poll cycle.

ideas.title: The Desk Screen Is a Silent Character -- Let It React
ideas.description: In Office.jsx, DESK_SCREEN_SVG has meticulously crafted per-worker screens: Carlos's blue editor lines, Maya's purple wireframe boxes, Dave's orange blinking terminal cursor, Sofia's green bar charts. These are beautiful and they never change -- a worker can be tagged Bug with error status and their screen still shows steady blue progress lines. The key is determined by worker.id or worker.id + '_error'. Extend this to task-aware lookup: build the screen key as worker.id + '_' + dominant task tag, falling back progressively to worker.id + '_error' then worker.id. At minimum add a sofia_qa variant (pulsing test pass/fail columns) and a maya_design variant (denser wireframe grid with more boxes). The DESK_SCREEN_SVG object already has error variants per worker -- the same pattern applies to task-state variants. WHY: the desk screen is the only element in the office that shows WHAT someone is working on spatially. Right now it just shows who they are. Let it show both.

ideas.title: The Lone Survivor Reads the Vibe, Not the Person
ideas.description: In Office.jsx, LONE_SURVIVOR_LABELS maps vibeKey to a label: 'on-fire' -> '🧯 one-person fireteam', 'crushing' -> '🎯 solo run'. This fires when isLoneSurvivor is true. Sofia holding the line during on-fire and Carlos holding the line during on-fire read identically. Sofia is QA -- she is catching regressions. Carlos is Backend -- he is keeping the db alive. Create LONE_SURVIVOR_WORKER_OVERRIDES keyed by worker.id with vibeKey sub-keys: carlos -> { 'on-fire': '🧯 backend won’t fall today', 'crushing': '📊 no bottlenecks when you ARE the stack' }, sofia -> { 'on-fire': '🔍 holding quality with both hands', 'crushing': '✅ bugs have nowhere to hide' }, dave -> { 'on-fire': '🎧 incident music: selected', 'slow-day': '🎵 coding into the void' }, maya -> { 'crushing': '💜 shipping pixels at terminal velocity' }, luna -> { 'after-hours': '🌙 reviews don’t stop at midnight' }. Fall back to LONE_SURVIVOR_LABELS[vibe] if no override exists. WHY: the lone survivor moment is the most cinematic frame in the product -- it should name the person, not just the vibe.

ideas.title: deep-work Has No Human Announcement
ideas.description: In Office.jsx, the char--deep-work CSS class fires when a worker has been actively working for 45+ minutes. This triggers a CSS animation change but there is no textual signal -- the Character component data-tooltip shows the same generic tooltip as any other working character. Add a DEEP_WORK_LABELS map keyed by worker.id: carlos -> 'deep in the stack', maya -> 'in the design zone', dave -> 'headphones: noise canceling', sofia -> 'thorough mode: activated', luna -> 'creative deep dive'. When variant === 'working' and updated_at is 45+ minutes ago, use this label in the tooltip: firstName + ' · ' + DEEP_WORK_LABELS[worker.id] + ' · ' + formatDuration(worker.updated_at). Consider also a tiny inline indicator alongside the typing-dots so deep work is visible without hover. WHY: 45 minutes of uninterrupted focus is a meaningful signal that should announce itself with each worker's own flavor rather than a generic CSS class that only designers ever notice.

ideas.title: Consolidate Five Voice Systems Into One Character Bible
ideas.description: Character voice is currently fragmented across at least five independent constants in two files: PING_REACTIONS (Office.jsx), AWAY_MESSAGES (Office.jsx), IDLE_BUBBLE_EMOJIS (Office.jsx), ERROR_BUBBLE_MESSAGES (Office.jsx), and the proposed additions from this review. Each was built independently and speaks in a different register. The result is a patchwork personality instead of a coherent internal voice for each worker. Proposal: create src/utils/characterVoice.js with a single CHARACTER_VOICE object keyed by worker.id with sub-keys for each context: { carlos: { idle: [...], error: [...], ping: '...', deepWork: '...', awayNotes: [...], cardTagline: '...' } }. Migrate all existing per-worker voice constants to import from this file. No rendering logic changes -- just one authoritative source of truth. Start with carlos and sofia as the first two entries as proof of concept. WHY: every future feature that wants to add character personality currently has to invent a new standalone constant in a new place. A character bible means the voice is an asset you can extend, not a side effect you have to find.

theme: The details share a wall — make them notice each other
visual_observations: After Hours vibe is live: Bloberto sits at CISO nameplate with a 🕯️, the sky label reads '🌙 Night sky — the stars are watching', and the door shows '🔒 Locked'. The office is mostly empty — Sofia and Luna at desks, three ghosts at top row. One orange/terracotta rectangle renders oddly large at roughly 85%/55% in the office canvas (the decorative plant SVG pot is rendering without clipping containment on some zoom levels). The conference table shows 'locked up' in lowercase near the bottom — it reads like a code comment, not atmosphere. The desk nameplates for idle workers all read '↪ away' with zero identity. The solo spotlight on the Dashboard says the exact same hardcoded sentence regardless of who is alone. On mobile, the office floor is compact but readable; the vibe weather label clips at the bottom edge on 375px width.

ideas.title: Solo Spotlight Message Doesn't Know Who's Alone
ideas.description: In App.jsx line ~1185, the `solo-spotlight-msg` paragraph is a hardcoded string: '🌟 Running solo today. Carrying the whole team on one pair of hands.' It fires regardless of who the solo worker is, their role, their vibe, or their progress percentage. The codebase already has `WORKER_TAGLINES` (App.jsx line ~258), `LONE_SURVIVOR_WORKER_OVERRIDES` (Office.jsx line ~106), and `LONE_SURVIVOR_LABELS` keyed by vibe (Office.jsx line ~101). Compose a `SOLO_SPOTLIGHT_MESSAGES` map: `{ carlos: { 'on-fire': '📊 One backend between the servers and chaos.', 'crushing': '🧱 The whole stack is one person right now.' }, sofia: { 'on-fire': '🔍 Holding quality with both hands and no backup.', 'slow-day': '🔎 If there are bugs, she will find them. Alone.' }, dave: { 'after-hours': '🎧 One DevOps engineer. Infinite deployment anxiety.' }, maya: { 'crushing': '💜 Shipping pixels at terminal velocity, unsupervised.' }, luna: { 'after-hours': '🌙 Creative review never clocks out.' } }`. Fall back to the existing generic string for unmatched combos. The `activeWorkers[0].id` and `teamVibe.key` are both in scope at the solo spotlight render site.

ideas.title: Idle Desk Nameplate Says '↪ away' for Every Single Person
ideas.description:
In Office.jsx line ~1373: `
↪ away
`. This renders for every idle worker sitting at their desk — Carlos, Maya, Dave, Sofia, Luna all get the same generic tag. The codebase already has `PING_REACTIONS` (Office.jsx line ~139) and `WORKER_TAGLINES` (App.jsx line ~258) establishing per-worker voice. Add a `WORKER_AWAY_DESK_LABELS` constant in Office.jsx: `{ carlos: ' logs', maya: '↪ sketching', dave: '↪ headphones', sofia: '↪ testing', luna: '↪ reviewing' }`. Replace the hardcoded string at line ~1373 with `{WORKER_AWAY_DESK_LABELS[occ.worker.id] ?? '↪ away'}`. Three words, zero effort, the desk starts knowing its person.

ideas.title: Door Label Has Two States for Five Vibes
ideas.description: In Office.jsx line ~1452, the door label renders: `{vibe === 'after-hours' ? 'Locked' : 'Entrance'}`. Five vibes, two outcomes. The door is the first and last thing the office communicates — it should speak the room's energy. Replace with a `DOOR_LABELS` map: `{ crushing: 'All hands in', 'on-fire': 'Brace yourself', 'in-flow': 'Focus zone', 'slow-day': 'Come on in', 'after-hours': 'Locked up' }` and render `{DOOR_LABELS[vibe] ?? 'Entrance'}`. The `on-fire` door should feel like a warning. The `crushing` door should feel like a rally cry. Right now every non-after-hours vibe reads 'Entrance' — which is the emotional equivalent of a blank wall.

ideas.title: Dashboard Tab Error Badge Counts But Never Escalates
ideas.description: In App.jsx line ~1198: `{errorCount > 0 && {errorCount}}`. The badge just displays a number. But the StatsBar already has a dramatic narrative system — `STAT_DISPATCHES.error` maps 0→'smooth sailing', 1→'minor turbulence', 2→'code red', 3→'everything is fine 🔥'. The tab badge should mirror that energy visually. Add `tab-error-badge--critical` CSS class when `errorCount >= 2` (pulse animation, brighter red), and `tab-error-badge--meltdown` at `errorCount >= 3` (frantic strobe at 0.4s interval). The number alone is data; the animation is context. The user's eye goes to the tab before the stats bar.

ideas.title: The Office Window Doesn't Know What's Happening Inside
ideas.description: In Office.jsx, `WindowElement` (~line 946) renders a time-of-day sky gradient using `new Date().getHours()` — sunrise, daylight, golden hour, dusk, midnight. Beautiful, correct, completely isolated from team state. The window is mounted two divs away from `data-vibe` on `office-floor`. Add a `vibeKey` prop to `WindowElement`. Apply a vibe-tinted CSS overlay inside `.office-window__sky`: `on-fire` → a faint red/orange wash (`rgba(239,68,68,0.12)`), `crushing` → a gold shimmer layer, `after-hours` → slightly deeper indigo, `slow-day` → a desaturated cool wash. This is a CSS `::after` pseudo-element on `.office-window__sky[data-vibe='on-fire']` — one line of CSS per vibe. The window looks outside but should feel inside.

ideas.title: Bloberto's Desk Monitor Has No Screen Content
ideas.description: In Office.jsx line ~1228, the `mgr-desk__monitor` renders as a styled `div` with zero inner content. Meanwhile, `DESK_SCREEN_SVG` (Office.jsx line ~10) provides rich per-worker SVG screens for all five employees — Carlos has blue code lines, Maya has a wireframe layout, Dave has a terminal cursor, Sofia has a QA bar chart, Luna has kanban columns. Bloberto — the CISO/CFO/CVO/CDO — sits in front of a blank monitor. Add a `BLOBERTO_MONITOR_SVG` object keyed by vibe: `crushing` → tiny upward bar chart in purple/gold, `on-fire` → flatline with a blinking alert indicator, `in-flow` → a smooth sine wave, `slow-day` → a single dot on a vast grid, `after-hours` → a screensaver with slow-drifting stars. Render inside `.mgr-desk__monitor` using `vibe` as the key. The boss deserves a dashboard.

ideas.title: Conference Table Ambient Label Reads Like a Code Comment
ideas.description: In Office.jsx, `ConferenceTable` (~line 864) renders the ambient label when no meeting is in progress: `vibe === 'after-hours' ? 'locked up' : vibe === 'crushing' ? 'everyone is at their desk' : vibe === 'on-fire' ? 'no meetings. fix it.' : vibe === 'in-flow' ? 'reserve the table' : 'open office hours'`. These are mostly fine, but 'locked up' reads like a commented-out variable name, not atmosphere. Rewrite: `'after-hours': '🌙 the table sleeps'`, `'slow-day': '📅 open hours — no RSVP needed'`. Also 'everyone is at their desk' for `crushing` is accurate but cold — try `'🚀 everyone's shipping, no time to sit'`. These labels are the caption for the entire social life of the office; they should feel like the narrator, not the docstring.

ideas.title: The Whiteboard Doodle Has No Author — Give It a Signature
ideas.description:
In Office.jsx line ~1249, `
` renders vibe-keyed ASCII art: `crushing` = `╰(*°▽°*)╯`, `on-fire` = `(╯°□°)╯︵ ┻━┻`, `in-flow` = `(⌐■_■)`, `slow-day` = `(._. )`, `after-hours` = `(∪.∪ )...zzz`. These are perfect. But they have no author. After the `pre`, add a tiny attribution line: a `DOODLE_AUTHORS` map `{ crushing: '— maya', 'on-fire': '— dave', 'in-flow': '— carlos', 'slow-day': '— sofia', 'after-hours': '— luna' }`. Render as `{DOODLE_AUTHORS[vibe]}` — styled in `Office.css` at `font-size: 0.3rem; opacity: 0.45; font-style: italic`. One line below the doodle. Now someone drew on the whiteboard. The office started having last night.
